title: How to size a compost system for your garden
subtitle: Matching bin volume to household waste output so the system actually works
excerpt: A bin that is too small fills before material can decompose and gets abandoned. A bin that is too large never heats up. The right size depends on three numbers: the household's weekly food waste output, the garden area, and whether hot or cold composting is the goal.
sourceNotes: Garden Organic composting system design guidance. WRAP household food waste generation data for UK households. Centre for Alternative Technology compost bin sizing notes.

glossaryTerms.definition: An actively managed compost heap that reaches internal temperatures of 55-70 degrees C through microbial activity. Hot composting kills weed seeds and pathogens and produces finished compost in 8-12 weeks.

content.text: Most composting guides recommend a bin or heap of at least 1 cubic metre for a family garden. This is the minimum for a cold heap to retain moisture and for a hot heap to insulate itself against heat loss. But the right volume for a specific household depends on inputs: the volume of food and garden waste generated per year.
The two main inputs are kitchen waste and garden waste.

A two-person household generates approximately 3-4 litres of compostable kitchen waste per week (vegetable peelings, fruit waste, coffee grounds, eggshells, tea bags). A family of four generates 5-8 litres per week. Over a year this is roughly 150-400 litres of kitchen input.

Garden waste volume is highly seasonal. A medium garden (50-100 m2 of planted beds plus a lawn) generates approximately 200-600 litres of compostable garden waste per year: grass clippings, spent plants, soft prunings, and leaf fall. Most of this arrives in two peaks, spring-summer (grass and annual plant waste) and autumn (leaf fall).

Raw compostable material shrinks by 50-70 percent during decomposition. A household generating 600 litres of input per year will produce roughly 200-300 litres of finished compost. The bin needs to hold the peak accumulation before it reduces. In practice, a single-bin cold system needs a capacity roughly equal to the total annual input. A two-bin or three-bin system can use smaller individual bins because one bin is always finishing while another is filling.
Small household, small garden, cold composting: one bin of 200-400 litres is sufficient for kitchen waste and light garden waste.
Family of four, medium garden, cold composting: one or two bins of 600-900 litres each.
bin should be at least 0.8 m3 (800 litres) in volume. Below this the heap loses heat faster than the microbial community generates it and the temperature never reaches the thermophilic zone.

Leaf mould only: a separate wire cage of 1-1.5 m3 handles the autumn leaf crop. Leaves take a year to break down and take up a large volume. They have low nitrogen, so they work better as a separate heap than mixed with kitchen or lawn waste.
Signs that the system is the wrong size
Bin fills to the top within two to three months and material is still recognisable: bin is too small for the input volume. Add a second bin or use a council green waste collection for garden waste.
Bin is never more than a quarter full: input volume is too low for this bin size. A worm bin or kitchen caddy may suit the household better, or the bin itself is too large.
Hot composting never heats: the heap is too small, or fresh material is being added too slowly to build the mass needed to reach high temperatures.
